comments.body: <T extends Enum> is not "extending" Enum, it says this generic type can be of type Enum or a subclass... it's strictly for type matching bounds.

answers.body:
Enum type is final in java architecture desgin you shouldn't create a class that extends this type in your project.

Reference:

link1

link2

Since Spring 3.0, the scenario you're describing can be covered with an implementation of the ConverterFactory interface. ConverterFactory is suitable when you need to centralize the conversion logic for a whole class hierarchy, like Enum, and return a different Converter based on the subtype you need to convert to, for example TimeUnit, DayOfWeek, or others.

The declaration of the ConverterFactory interface is the following, where:

  • S is the type to convert from. In your case: String.
  • R is the base type to convert to. In your case: Enum.
  • T is a subtype of R and the actual type to convert to. In your case, any of your custom enums in upper case.
public interface ConverterFactory<S, R> {
    <T extends R> Converter<S, T> getConverter(Class<T> targetType);
}

Your implementation could look like this:

final class StringToEnumConverterFactory implements ConverterFactory<String, Enum> {

    public <T extends Enum> Converter<String, T> getConverter(Class<T> targetType) {
        return new StringToEnumConverter<>(targetType);
    }

    private final class StringToEnumConverter<T extends Enum> implements Converter<String, T> {

        private final Class<T> enumType;

        public StringToEnumConverter(Class<T> enumType) {
            this.enumType = enumType;
        }

        public T convert(String source) {
            return (T) Enum.valueOf(this.enumType, source.trim().toUpperCase());
        }
    }
}

Here is a possible example:

public enum MyEnum {
    MY_ENUM1, MY_ENUM2, MY_ENUM3;
}

public enum MyOtherEnum {
    MY_OTHER_ENUM1, MY_OTHER_ENUM2, MY_OTHER_ENUM3;
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StringToEnumConverterFactory converterFactory = new StringToEnumConverterFactory();
        MyEnum myEnum1 = converterFactory.getConverter(MyEnum.class).convert("MY_ENUM1");
        MyOtherEnum myOtherEnum2 = converterFactory.getConverter(MyOtherEnum.class).convert("MY_OTHER_ENUM2");
    }
}

Here is a link to the paragraph Using ConverterFactory of the Spring Framework Tutorial to further the topic on converters.

I want to add some details to the dani-vita's answer:

  1. The OP code doesn't work because of type erasure. The converter factory is a "trick" to overcome this problem.
  2. In Spring Boot, you have to register the converter factory, as Baeldung reports:
@Configuration
public class WebConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    @Override
    public void addFormatters(final FormatterRegistry registry) {
        registry.addConverterFactory(new StringToEnumConverterFactory());
    }
}

Notice that you don't need to register also the converter as Baeldung does. It's impossible, since it's private, and useless, because of type erasure and because the converter needs to be instantiated with the class type.

Furthermore, you don't need the @Component annotation. The @Component annotation works out of the box only for Converters.

Tested with Spring Boot 3.5.x

title: Spring generic (parameterized type) conversion service?
body:
I'm trying to create a generic Converter for Spring that will allow incoming Enum values to be mixed case and converted toUpperCase() prior to mapping to the enum.

The conversion works well for any specific Enum type we're using when the Converter is defined for a specific enum type. However, when I write this as a generic Spring never calls the code.

import java.lang.reflect.ParameterizedType;
import java.lang.reflect.Type;
import org.springframework.core.convert.converter.Converter;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

// Doesn't work, Spring doesn't appear to allow Converters with type parameters :-(
@Component
public class StringToEnumConverter <T extends Enum> implements Converter<String, T> {

    @Override
    public T convert(String string) {
        Type type = ((ParameterizedType)StringToEnumConverter.class.getGenericSuperclass()).getActualTypeArguments()[0];
        return (T) Enum.valueOf((Class<T>)type, string.trim().toUpperCase());
    }
}

Is the code incorrect or is it that Spring doesn't know how to match the type at runtime? I was hoping that T extends Enum is enough information for Spring to send all Enum subclasses through here.
